linkedin.text:
The CMO isn't dying. The CMO is being reforged.

Forrester says B2B CMO representation dropped from 48% to 42%. Spencer Stuart says tenure ticked up to 4.3 years. Gartner says budgets are flat at 7.7% of revenue while scope keeps expanding.

Read those four data points together and a picture forms. It's not a role disappearing. It's a role that has outgrown its container.

The traditional CMO — the one who owns brand and demand gen — is increasingly irrelevant. Not because the work doesn't matter. Because the work has expanded so far beyond "marketing" that the title itself has become a constraint.

What's replacing it is someone who orchestrates the entire GTM motion. Across functions. Across humans and machines. Across the full customer lifecycle.

I wrote about this shift — what it means for org design, why sales might report into it, and what changes when you replace campaign spikes with rivers of predictable impact.

Full piece on Strategnik:
linkedin.title: The CMO Is Dead. Long Live the Chief Marketing Orchestrator

thread:
The CMO isn't dying. It's being reforged into something harder.

Forrester: B2B CMO representation dropped from 48% to 42%.
Spencer Stuart: Tenure ticked up to 4.3 years.
Gartner: Budgets flat at 7.7% while scope keeps expanding.

Read those together. A thread 🧵

The traditional CMO — owns brand, demand gen, maybe product marketing — is increasingly irrelevant.

Not because the work doesn't matter.

Because the work has expanded so far beyond "marketing" that the title itself has become a constraint.

What's replacing it: a leader who orchestrates the entire GTM motion.

Across functions.
Across humans and machines.
Across the full customer lifecycle.

I'm calling it the Chief Marketing Orchestrator.

The biggest shift: replacing campaign spikes with rivers of predictable impact.

Campaigns produce feast-or-famine cycles.
Rivers compound.

Momentum > activity.

Full piece: https://strategnik.com/thinking/chief-marketing-orchestrator/
